schools.summary: Cyril: the ministers of teaching work miracles as a credential of their divine sending, bringing down the devil's pride; Eusebius: power to drive out spirits and cure diseases.
Chrysostom: taking nothing held them aloof from care, that they might give their whole study to the word.
The indirect middle 'called together' (Mark 6:7; Matthew 10:1); Christ, in but one place, sends the Twelve abroad (Henry).
The present infinitives mark continuous functions; the double office of herald and healer (Matthew 10:7-8); Luke uses iaomai and therapeuein interchangeably.
Gill expounds 'the kingdom of God' as the gospel of the Messiah's kingdom of grace and glory.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the fruits of repentance, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The better text 'neither a staff' (singular), the plural adopted to harmonise with Matthew 10:10 / Mark 6:8 (where Mark allows a staff); winter coming yet no second garment (Lightfoot).
Clarke: remain in that lodging till you depart from the city (Matthew 10:11); some MSS add 'depart not thence'; the house entered for lodging during the stay (Gill).
The indefinite relative with subjunctive; 'for a testimony against them' (ep' autous) like Mark's dative of disadvantage (Mark 6:11); the shaken dust recalls Acts 13:51.
The imperfect 'went' with three present participles describes the wide, repeated work through all the villages of Galilee; 'villages' for 'towns' (Ellicott).

outliers.note: The note here concerns the Benedictus, not this verse.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: it was long before the tyrant took notice; sinners fear both knowing and ignorant, afraid of shadows; Theophylact distinguishes this Herod from his father.
The imperfect 'was much perplexed' (diaporei, only in Luke); the three reports — John risen, Elijah, a prophet; Clarke's metaphor of the traveller at many paths.
The recurring 'one of the old prophets risen again' (v.19; John 1:21); Elijah, translated alive, expected to 'appear' before Messiah; he 'could not rise, because he did not die' (Wesley).
The imperfect 'he kept on seeking to see Jesus', disturbed because sure he had beheaded John; Luke returns to this desire at Luke 23:8, from sources unique to him.

Isidore of Pelusium: after John's murder the Lord left the murderers and departed, sparing his enemies and awaiting his hour; Ambrose: heavenly nourishment given to those who seek Christ in the desert.
Bethsaida (Julias) peculiar to Luke, across the sea (John 6:1); 'told him all' a circumstantial narration; the retirement for rest (Henry).
The imperfects 'continued speaking' and 'continued healing'; the word for healing (almost only in Luke) more technical, like 'treatment'.
Clarke: five grand effects of grace — the people drawn, received, instructed, healed, and fed; Jesus is still the same to all who follow him.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the fruits of repentance, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: 'To wear away' (klinein, the sun setting), intransitive only in Luke; 'lodge' (katalysosin) of travellers breaking a journey; 'the villages and farms' (Ellicott); full account in Matthew 14:15.

outliers.note: The block here comments on the sending of the Twelve (vv.1-5), a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: The third-class condition 'except we should go and buy food'; 'food' (bromata) = eaten pieces; Christ would supply them from the disciples' own little stock (Gill).
Luke's 'about' (hosei) with the 5,000 men of Mark 6:44; 'sit down' (recline) in 'companies' (klisias, table-companies) of about fifty; all done in order (1 Corinthians 14:40).
Gill: the disciples disputed no longer but obeyed, ranging the multitude in companies and seating them on the green grass.
The single aorist 'brake' (kateklasen, only here) followed by the imperfect 'kept on giving' (edidou), the loaves multiplying in his hands as he distributed.
Clarke: the minister's lesson — take the bread of life himself, lift his soul to God, rightly divide the word to feed others.
All filled, every one with enough (Gill); the hungry filled (Psalm 107:9); twelve baskets (kophinoi) of broken pieces (Matthew 14:20).
Cyril: the Lord praying apart ordained himself an example; the subtle skill of the question, leading first through others' opinions; Ambrose: enough to know Christ crucified.
The Lukan idiom of prayer (Christ alone here, cf. Luke 3:21); Henry: he found time to be alone in prayer; Luke omits the walking on water and the four thousand (Ellicott).

outliers.note: The block here comments on the feeding (vv.12-17), a catena offset, not this verse.
The note here is misplaced, not bearing on the verse.

schools.summary: The accusative-and-infinitive 'that I am', where Matthew 16:13 has 'the Son of man'; the opinions parallel to the rumours that reached Herod (vv.7-8).

The emphatic proleptic 'ye' (hymeis de) — what mattered now; the confession a settled conviction, not a new discovery; 'the Christ of God' (peculiar to Luke, cf. Luke 2:26; the fuller form in Matthew 16:16).
He ceased using 'Messiah' of himself because of its political meaning; its use would lead to revolution (John 6:15); though he blessed Peter's confession, he charged them to silence (Gill).
'Rejected' (apodokimasthenai, after trial); 'the third day' (Matthew 16:21) and Mark's 'after three days' (Mark 8:31) the same in sense; the 'must' of divine necessity.
Cyril: the disciples too must pass through like struggles; Basil: 'come after me' a due imitation of his life, self-denial a forsaking of one's own will.
Bede: none comes near to Christ but he who first denies himself, taking up the cross to follow.
Luke alone has 'daily' (omitted in many MSS and the parallels, Matthew 16:24; Mark 8:34); 'he said unto all' — the lesson for the multitude too.

debates.question: Is 'daily' part of the original text?

sides.position: Peculiar to Luke; omitted in many MSS and the parallels, but true in sense

outliers.note: The block here comments on Peter's confession (vv.18-22), a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: The saying widely attested (Matthew 16:25; Mark 8:35; John 12:25); to save one's life by denying Christ is to lose it eternally, to lose it for him is to save it (Gill).

outliers.note: A Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: Clarke: temporal damage the world might repay, but spiritual damage the universe could not indemnify; Ellicott: better 'destroy himself, or suffer loss'.
Gill: all the world's honours and riches are of no service if a man himself is lost.

'Whosoever is ashamed' (Mark 8:38), shame in the eyes of men, while Christ endured the cross's shame (Hebrews 12:2) and Paul was not ashamed (Romans 1:16); 'in his own glory' peculiar to Luke (John 17:5).

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the resurrection (Jonah), tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The idiom 'till they see' and the rabbinical 'shall not taste of death' (Matthew 16:28; Mark 9:1, which adds 'come with power'); 'Christ tasted death that we may see how to die'; placed before the Transfiguration.

outliers.note: The block here comments on the self-denial sayings (vv.23-26), a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: Eusebius: he proceeds from words to works, manifesting the image of his kingdom; Damascene harmonises Matthew/Mark's 'six days' with Luke's 'eight' as no disagreement.
Bede: he ascends to pray and be transfigured, that those who would see the King in glory keep their hearts on high; Ambrose reads the three as the human race.
'About eight days' agrees with 'after six days' (Mark 9:2), Luke counting both end days; the mountain probably Hermon near Caesarea Philippi.

debates.question: Luke's 'about eight days' vs Matthew/Mark's 'after six days'?

sides.position: No disagreement — Luke counts both the first and last day

schools.summary: Luke's stress on Christ praying (Luke 3:21); the most classical Evangelist avoids 'transfigured' (metamorphosed) as too pagan, saying 'the fashion of his countenance was altered'; the glistering whiteness (Mark 9:3).
Lightfoot cites the Jewish expectation that Moses would come with Elijah; Moses represents the Law, Elijah the Prophets, both in converse with Christ.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the Benedictus, not this verse.

schools.summary: 'Decease' literally exodus — a departure covering death, resurrection, and ascension, with an allusion to Israel's exodus (2 Peter 1:15); the variant 'glory' in some MSS; the subject named only by Luke.

themes: the glory points to the cross

schools.summary: Theophylact: the sleep natural weakness; Chrysostom: a daze from the overwhelming light, not night; the disciples wake to see his glory and Moses and Elijah.
Ambrose: the divine brightness oppresses the bodily senses, as the sun overpowers the eye — the frailty of flesh before God's glory.

debates.question: What is the 'heavy sleep' of the disciples?

sides.position: A daze from the overwhelming brightness of the vision

schools.summary: Robertson: Luke's 'as they were departing,' the varied titles (Master/Rabbi/Lord), 'let us make' (Peter for the three); Barnes: 'it is good to be here' (Psalm 27:4) yet 'not knowing what he said' — a sincere but confused impulse to detain the glory.

themes: it is good to be here

schools.summary: Robertson: Luke's inchoative 'overshadowed' (a cloud covering a mountain cap), the same verb as the Spirit on Mary (Luke 1:35); the three feared entering the cloud.
Chrysostom: the bright cloud (not dark) received Christ as the Lord dwelt in the cloud of old — a witness to his equality with the Father.

themes: the cloud of the divine presence

schools.summary: Robertson: the Father's voice (as at the baptism, Luke 3:22); Ellicott/Clarke: the better text reads 'my chosen' (not 'beloved,' likely harmonized to the others); Barnes: 'hear him' fulfils Moses' prophecy (Deuteronomy 18:15) — Christ supreme above the law and prophets.

debates.question: Does the voice say 'my beloved Son' or 'my chosen Son'?

sides.position: 'My chosen' (the better MSS); 'beloved' harmonized to Matthew/Mark

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: the voice past, Jesus found alone (Moses and Elijah gone), in his ordinary form — the Son alone to be heard; the disciples kept it secret 'in those days' until the resurrection.

Bede: the contrast of mount and valley — above the Father's voice and transfiguration, below the casting out of devils; Christ descends from glory to need; Titus: the father's wise plea ('Look on my son').

Ellicott: 'mine only child' Luke's tender touch (as the widow of Nain, Luke 7:12); Robertson: 'look upon' a medical word for examining, monogenēs in Luke alone (7:12; 8:42) — the father's humble plea that Christ merely regard his son.

Gill: a real evil spirit seizing and possessing the boy; Clarke's Herodotus parallel ('the God possesses us') showing the ancient language of demonic seizure; symptoms resembling epilepsy yet traced to genuine possession.

Gill: the nine left below failed though given power over demons; Barnes: like those who used Christ's name without his power (Acts 19:13-16); the failure (the Gospels say) from unbelief and prayerlessness — magnifying Christ's contrasting power.
Gill: the rebuke aimed at the unbelieving Jews and gloating scribes, not the disciples whose fault was lesser.

Robertson: Luke combines Mark's two questions; 'faithless' (disbelieving), 'perverse' (twisted); Barnes: echoing Moses' 'perverse generation' (Deuteronomy 32:5) — Christ's grief at unbelief, ending in mercy ('bring thy son').

debates.question: Whom does Christ rebuke as 'faithless and perverse'?

sides.position: The unbelieving Jews and scribes, not chiefly the disciples
The whole faithless generation, including the disciples' want of faith

themes: how long shall I suffer you

schools.summary: Robertson: the demon's last violent assault, Christ's rebuke and instant cure where the nine failed; 'gave him back to his father' a tender touch alone in Luke (cf. 7:15); all astonished at the easy victory.

Cyril: amid the crowd's wonder Christ warns the disciples alone of his passion, lest they be perplexed and doubt his deity; Titus: miracles do not save, but the cross conveys the benefit — glory tempered by the cross.

themes: amazed at the mighty power of God

schools.summary: Gill: a solemn charge to lay up deep what he is about to say (the passion-prediction); Cyril: the mystery of the passion deposited in the disciples (not the crowd); Barnes: or 'these sayings' = the miracles/praise to remember when he dies.

debates.question: What are 'these sayings' to sink down?

sides.position: The passion-prediction itself, to fix in memory
The recent miracles and the people's confession

schools.summary: Gill: they could not reconcile a suffering Messiah with their notion of a victorious temporal Redeemer; Barnes: hid 'not by God but by their false preconceptions' — error blinds men to the plainest truth; they feared to ask, shrinking from the unwelcome cross.
Cyril: the devil, failing by fleshly lust, ensnares the godly by vain-glory — a subtler snare; the evangelist names no individual, perhaps not all shared it.
Theophylact: the strife sprang from the failed exorcism (mutual blame); Bede: jealousy of the three on the mount and Peter's keys — pride intruding right after the passion-prediction.

debates.question: What occasioned the dispute over greatness?

sides.position: The failed exorcism and mutual blame
Jealousy of the three honoured on the mount

schools.summary: Gill: Christ knew their ambition not by hearing but as the omniscient discerner of hearts, the dispute carried on privately.
Robertson: he set the child by his side (Mark 'in his arms') as an object-lesson in the humility they sorely needed — the lowly child the pattern of greatness.

themes: perceiving the thought of their heart

schools.summary: Gill: 'this child' = the childlike believer (meek, humble, unambitious), not youth; to receive such is to receive Christ.
Ellicott: better text 'is great' — greatness a present reality attained through humility, not a future reward; Barnes: the kingdom's reversal (Matthew 23:11-12) — the least is the greatest.

themes: he that is least is great

schools.summary: John, prompted by 'in my name,' reports forbidding a man who cast out devils in Christ's name but 'followeth not with us' — a sectarian zeal restricting Christ's work to their own circle, ironic after the strife over greatness.

themes: one casting out devils in thy name

schools.summary: Robertson: 'forbid him not' (stop hindering); 'not against us is for us' a proverb, paired with the converse (Luke 11:23, 'not with me is against me') for different cases; the lesson of toleration in methods of work for Christ, needed still.

themes: not against us is for us
Christ's cause wider than one party

schools.summary: Cyril: the time near for his Passion and ascension, he resolves on Jerusalem; Titus: the true Lamb offered where the typical was, going straight there; 'received up' embracing passion, resurrection, ascension.
Bede: the steadfast advance to a foreseen cross proves Christ God, not a mere man — going voluntarily and foreknowing to be crucified (cf. Isaiah 50:7).

themes: the time he should be received up
the voluntary march to the cross

schools.summary: Barnes/Clarke: 'messengers' = literally 'angels,' showing the word means any messenger, not only heavenly beings; Christ's prudent forethought to make ready; Lightfoot on Jewish-Samaritan enmity (hatred, not real danger of defilement); Gill: perhaps James and John sent.

Barnes: rejected over the Jerusalem/Gerizim temple dispute — his journey implied a verdict against them; Gill: they would have welcomed him had he honoured their worship; sectarian prejudice closing the heart against the Saviour.

Barnes: the 'sons of thunder' (Mark 3:17), rash zeal — vengeance belongs to God; Clarke: they appeal to Elijah's fire (2 Kings 1:10-12); Ellicott: anger perhaps sharpened by Christ's former welcome among Samaritans (John 4) — sincere love but a carnal, vengeful spirit.

themes: wilt thou that we command fire

schools.summary: Barnes: apparent zeal for God may be revenge against men — examine the spirit behind professed zeal; Lightfoot: the disciples failed to catch their tender Master's spirit, proposing slaughter where he healed and spared; Christ's searching rebuke.

themes: ye know not what manner of spirit

schools.summary: Clarke/Ellicott: much of vv.55-56 absent from the best MSS (Griesbach omits), perhaps inserted from oral tradition preserving Christ's words; the sentiment true — Christ came to save not destroy (John 3:17); his patient withdrawal to another village.

debates.question: Are the words 'the Son of man is not come to destroy but to save' part of the original text?

sides.position: Absent from the best MSS, perhaps a true tradition added later
Genuine in sense, expressing Christ's mission

themes: not come to destroy but to save

schools.summary: Cyril: presumption — grasping the honour of discipleship none takes uncalled (Hebrews 5:4); Athanasius: rashly matching the incomprehensible Christ; Theophylact: a mercenary hope of gain from the crowds — eager words without counting the cost.

debates.question: What was the flaw in the eager volunteer?

schools.summary: Gill: the same words as in Matthew; Wesley: 'understand the terms' — count the conditions of following; Christ lays bare his homelessness (less than foxes and birds), testing whether the man sought gain or would share his poverty and self-denial.
Gill: a disciple Christ now calls to the ministry, who pleads first to bury his father — putting a sacred duty 'first' before the call.
Cyril: Christ the head of every virtue calls men by the gospel into the apostolic path — a call above all rival claims.

themes: suffer me first to bury my father

schools.summary: Robertson: 'the spiritually dead bury the literally dead'; the harshness because the man used his aged father as a pretext to evade the call; Lightfoot on the Jewish reckoning of some 'as dead'; the gospel call overrides even burial.

debates.question: Why so sharp a word against a dutiful son?

sides.position: He used his father as a pretext to evade the clear call to preach

themes: let the dead bury their dead

schools.summary: Cyril contrasts the self-called first man with true following at Christ's call; the third man, like Elisha (1 Kings 19:20), asks a 'first' farewell — a seemingly modest request masking a divided, backward-looking heart.

Barnes: the ploughman must look forward — the disciple with a whole, undivided heart, not looking back to the world; 'religion is everything or nothing'; Clarke: the called minister cannot be entangled in secular pursuits — wholehearted, forward devotion required.
